name: pine plank stack
shortDescription: A clean stack of pine planks cut for quick repairs, cheap shelving, stall boards, and wagon patches. The timber is light, easy to plane, and never as impressive as oak, but every market quarter needs something that can be nailed into usefulness before sundown.
flavorText: “Pine is what merchants buy when the job is urgent and the customer has already spent the oak money.”

name: oak beam
shortDescription: A heavy oak beam squared for rafters, gateframes, bridge braces, or any project that expects to outlive the builder. It costs more to haul than to admire, but carpenters trust its stubborn grain when cheaper wood would bend, split, or apologize.
flavorText: “Oak does not hurry. That is why roofs trust it.”

name: barrel stave bundle
shortDescription: A tied bundle of shaped barrel staves, ready for a cooper with hoops, patience, and a mallet. They look plain on the cart, but turn into storage for ale, oil, grain, salt fish, and every other good that hates open air.
flavorText: “Before wine becomes profit, it needs a wooden promise around it.”

name: pitch bucket
shortDescription: A tar-dark bucket of sticky pitch used to seal hulls, patch roofs, waterproof seams, and ruin good sleeves. Dockhands buy it by necessity, not affection, because nothing else stops leaks with such ugly confidence.
flavorText: “Every ship smells a little of pitch, fear, and unpaid repair bills.”

name: slate tile stack
shortDescription: A flat stack of slate tiles split thin for roofs, hearth edges, and tidy shop signs. They are brittle in careless hands but dependable once laid, especially in towns where rain tests every poor choice by morning.
flavorText: “Clay cracks. Thatch drinks. Slate simply waits for the weather to get bored.”

name: iron nail keg
shortDescription: A small keg packed with iron nails of mixed practical lengths, the kind every carpenter, wagonwright, and desperate fence mender eventually asks for. No single nail is worth much, but a town without them comes apart one loose board at a time.
flavorText: “Civilization is beams, ropes, and an unreasonable number of nails.”

name: bog iron ore
shortDescription: A damp lump of bog iron ore pulled from marshy ground and sold before it stains everything nearby. It is crude, earthy, and troublesome to smelt, but village forges value a local source when mountain ore is late or taxed too hard.
flavorText: “Not all iron comes from proud hills. Some of it crawls out of mud.”

name: red iron ore
shortDescription: A rusty-red iron ore with enough promise to interest any smith willing to pay for fuel and labor. The color marks it clearly in a mixed wagon, and experienced buyers judge the weight, grit, and stain before arguing price.
flavorText: “Good ore blushes before the forge ever sees it.”

name: charcoal bundle
shortDescription: A tied bundle of dense charcoal favored by smiths, cooks, and anyone who needs steady heat without a mountain of smoke. It leaves black fingers and honest work behind, which is usually a fair sign in a merchant yard.
flavorText: “Coal makes a racket. Charcoal keeps its secrets hot.”

name: forge flux jar
shortDescription: A sealed jar of forge flux used to help stubborn metals join, clean, and behave under the hammer. Smiths treat it as a small thing until they run out, at which point every weld suddenly becomes a sermon on poor planning.
flavorText: “A pinch of flux can save more iron than a week of excuses.”

name: steel billet
shortDescription: A compact steel billet waiting to become blades, chisels, buckles, hinges, or whatever the buyer can afford to have hammered out of it. Plain and rectangular, it carries more potential than glamour, which is why smiths prefer it that way.
flavorText: “A billet is not a weapon, tool, or lock yet. That is exactly its value.”

name: brass bar
shortDescription: A straight brass bar with a warm yellow shine, useful for fittings, clasps, trim, instrument parts, and respectable decorative work. It is softer than iron but easier to flatter, making it popular among artisans who sell to customers with opinions.
flavorText: “Brass is what iron wears when it wants to be invited indoors.”

name: rivet tin
shortDescription: A small tin filled with rivets, sorted well enough for armor repair, strap work, hinges, and hurried wagon fixes. It rattles like loose coins, though every craftsperson knows the contents are worth more when something important starts coming apart.
flavorText: “A rivet is only noticed after it fails. Sensible people buy them before that.”

name: hinge blank
shortDescription: An unfinished hinge blank ready for shaping, drilling, and fitting to doors, chests, shutters, or gates. It saves a smith the first rough steps and saves the buyer from pretending that a squealing door has character.
flavorText: “Every door tells a story. Bad hinges make it scream.”

name: quenching oil jar
shortDescription: A heavy jar of quenching oil used by smiths to cool hot metal with less shock than water. It smells sharp, keeps its secrets under a dark surface, and can decide whether a blade emerges loyal or cracked.
flavorText: “Steel remembers the fire, but it takes its final mood from the quench.”

name: sea glass chips
shortDescription: A pouch of smooth sea glass chips in cloudy greens, blues, and pale whites, gathered from tide-worn scraps and sold to jewelers, mosaic makers, and children with patient eyes. Their value lies less in rarity than in the sea’s quiet polishing.
flavorText: “The ocean makes even broken things look deliberate.”

name: moonstone pebble
shortDescription: A small moonstone pebble with a milky inner glow that shifts when turned in the hand. It is too modest for crown work, but charm makers, ring setters, and romantic fools all know modest stones sell best under lamplight.
flavorText: “Some stones glitter. Moonstone listens.”

name: pearl seed
shortDescription: A tiny pearl seed used by jewelers, shell workers, and patient traders who know small luster can become fine ornament with the right setting. Buyers inspect it closely, because the difference between promise and grit is often just politeness.
flavorText: “The smallest pearls start the longest arguments.”

name: mother of pearl tile
shortDescription: A thin tile of mother of pearl with soft shifting color, cut for inlay on boxes, instruments, handles, or shrine work. It is delicate, luminous, and expensive enough that careful artisans count the pieces twice before breathing near them.
flavorText: “Shell remembers the tide even after a craftsman teaches it geometry.”

name: enamel powder jar
shortDescription: A lidded jar of colored enamel powder for jewelers, badge makers, and decorative metalworkers. In the jar it looks like dust; under heat and skill it becomes bright surfaces that can make a cheap clasp look courtly.
flavorText: “Powder for the patient, color for the fire.”

name: farrier rasp
shortDescription: A tough farrier rasp used to shape hooves and keep working animals sound on long roads. It is a practical tool, not a pretty one, but caravan masters pay quickly for anything that keeps a mule from limping at the wrong mile.
flavorText: “A kingdom may ride on banners, but a caravan rides on hooves.”

name: glassblower pipe
shortDescription: A long glassblower pipe with a heat-darkened working end and the faint smell of old furnaces. In trained hands it turns breath, molten glass, and timing into vessels; in untrained hands it mostly turns coin into regret.
flavorText: “Glass begins as fire and sand, then waits for someone steady enough to breathe shape into it.”

name: net mender shuttle
shortDescription: A smooth net mender shuttle used to repair fishing nets, cargo nets, and trap webbing with quick practiced loops. It is small enough to overlook until a torn net spills a day’s profit back into the water.
flavorText: “A net is only as useful as its worst knot.”

name: rope splicing fid
shortDescription: A tapered rope splicing fid for opening strands and weaving repairs into dock lines, rigging, and hauling ropes. Sailors prize a good one because a proper splice holds where a lazy knot would fail in the rain.
flavorText: “Knots confess under strain. Splices keep their mouths shut.”

name: ledger ruler
shortDescription: A straight ledger ruler used to keep columns, weights, and debts from wandering across the page. It has no magic, unless one counts the quiet terror it brings to customers who hoped the numbers might blur.
flavorText: “A crooked line in a ledger is how arguments learn to walk.”

name: brass compass
shortDescription: A brass compass with a steady needle and enough scuffs to prove it has already survived several owners. Navigators, surveyors, scouts, and cautious merchants all like it for the same reason: it argues less than maps do.
flavorText: “North is a small mercy when every road is trying to sell you a story.”

name: reed basket
shortDescription: A light reed basket woven for market produce, laundry, shellfish, or anything that benefits from air and dislikes heavy crates. It will not impress nobles, but it can carry a day’s trade without adding much weight to the seller’s arm.
flavorText: “A good basket makes humble goods look ready for market.”

name: waxed pouch
shortDescription: A small waxed pouch made to keep letters, herbs, coins, or dry tinder safe from rain and river spray. Travelers buy it after losing something once; experienced travelers buy two before leaving town.
flavorText: “Water teaches thrift by ruining whatever you forgot to wrap.”

name: blueglass bottle
shortDescription: A blueglass bottle with a narrow neck and a cool deep color that makes ordinary contents look slightly mysterious. Apothecaries and perfumers love it, while tavern keepers charge more whenever the glass looks expensive enough to suggest a secret recipe.
flavorText: “Blue glass makes even vinegar look like it came from a wizard’s shelf.”

name: clay amphora
shortDescription: A clay amphora built for oil, wine, grain, or preserved goods, with sturdy shoulders and a market-friendly shape. It is old technology because old technology often works, especially when roads are rough and warehouses are suspiciously damp.
flavorText: “Empires fall. Amphorae keep carrying olives.”

name: padded gem case
shortDescription: A small padded gem case lined to keep loose stones from scratching, chipping, or quietly vanishing into a larger chest. Jewelers value the case almost as much as the gems when the road gets bumpy and hands get curious.
flavorText: “A gem in cloth is cargo. A gem in a case is inventory.”

name: potion rack
shortDescription: A compact potion rack with slots sized for vials, flasks, and bottles that should absolutely not knock together in a moving wagon. Alchemists call it organization; everyone else calls it self-preservation.
flavorText: “The first rule of potions is to stop them from meeting each other too soon.”

name: trail lantern
shortDescription: A sturdy trail lantern with a guarded flame and a handle worn smooth by travel. It is not elegant, but it turns bad paths into visible bad paths, which is often the difference between a late arrival and a broken ankle.
flavorText: “A lantern does not shorten the road. It just tells you where it is lying.”

name: sunwax candle
shortDescription: A pale sunwax candle that burns cleanly with a warm, steady light favored by scribes, healers, and shrine keepers. It costs more than tallow, but it does not fill a room with the smell of regret.
flavorText: “Cheap candles reveal darkness. Good candles improve the room.”

name: road map
shortDescription: A folded road map marked with routes, inns, bridges, tolls, and a few optimistic shortcuts. The ink is practical rather than beautiful, which is exactly what a tired caravan wants when the clouds start gathering.
flavorText: “Every road map contains one lie. The trick is finding it before the wheels do.”

name: toll pass
shortDescription: A stamped toll pass used to move goods through a gate, bridge, ferry, or border checkpoint without paying twice. It is only paper until a guard asks for it; then it becomes the cheapest thing in the wagon.
flavorText: “A pass is a small rectangle that tells a large man to step aside.”

name: saddlebag pair
shortDescription: A matched pair of saddlebags with reinforced seams and enough road dust to look trustworthy. They are built for tools, rations, letters, samples, and all the small necessities that riders pretend they packed lightly.
flavorText: “No rider travels light. Some simply hide the weight better.”

name: caravan repair kit
shortDescription: A practical caravan repair kit stocked with patches, bindings, spare pins, small tools, and the kind of humble parts that save a trip from becoming a roadside camp. It sells best to merchants who have already learned the price of not having one.
flavorText: “The road breaks small things first, then waits to see who prepared.”

name: weather charm
shortDescription: A small weather charm carried by sailors, farmers, and nervous traders who know forecasts can be bargaining tools or prayers. It may not command the sky, but it gives its owner something to hold while watching clouds gather over unpaid cargo.
flavorText: “No charm owns the weather. Some merely make fear easier to carry.”

name: tar pot
shortDescription: A blackened tar pot used for rope, seams, roof patches, and all the sticky work no apprentice volunteers for twice. It is messy, pungent, and vital wherever water keeps trying to enter places it was not invited.
flavorText: “Tar is not elegant. Neither is a leak.”

name: sail patch kit
shortDescription: A sail patch kit with heavy cloth, cord, needles, and waxed thread for quick repairs before wind turns a tear into a disaster. Captains buy it as insurance, then complain about the price until the first storm.
flavorText: “The sea respects large ships and small stitches equally little.”

name: brass fishhook tin
shortDescription: A pocket tin of brass fishhooks, sorted by size for river, coast, and patient hunger. Fisherfolk value the shine and resistance to rust, while merchants value how quickly hooks disappear into pockets when left unattended.
flavorText: “Hooks catch fish, fingers, and occasionally honest profit.”

name: crab trap
shortDescription: A slatted crab trap built to lure shellfish in and make escape an embarrassing design problem. It smells faintly of salt and old bait, which buyers accept as proof that the thing has at least understood its profession.
flavorText: “A crab trap works by offering dinner and closing negotiations.”

name: tide chart
shortDescription: A tide chart marked with harbor rises, shoal dangers, and the hours when mud becomes water again. Dockmasters, smugglers, and honest captains all read it differently, but they all pay attention.
flavorText: “The tide keeps no appointments, yet every harbor works around its mood.”

name: signal flag
shortDescription: A bright signal flag used by ships, watchtowers, caravans, and market yards to say important things at inconvenient distances. Its meaning depends on color, pattern, and local habit, so wise buyers ask before waving it proudly.
flavorText: “A flag can save a ship or start an argument. Learn the difference.”

name: Ledger of Little Debts
shortDescription: A narrow ledger filled with small debts, favors, IOUs, and reminders that no coin is too modest to become a future problem. Shopkeepers love it because memory is generous, but ink is not.
flavorText: “Large debts make enemies. Little debts make regular customers.”

name: Tide Almanac
shortDescription: A well-thumbed tide almanac listing lunar tables, harbor notes, storm cautions, and the stubborn habits of coastal water. It is useful for sailors, fishers, ferrymen, and anyone who has ever watched profit drift out with the tide.
flavorText: “The sea changes daily, which is why sensible people write it down yearly.”

name: Orchard Road Recipes
shortDescription: A practical recipe booklet from the orchard roads, full of pies, preserves, stewed fruit, nut breads, and ways to stretch a harvest without insulting it. Bakers value the margins as much as the recipes, where generations of substitutions quietly live.
flavorText: “Every orchard family has a secret recipe and a public version for neighbors.”

name: Harbor Beasts and How to Price Them
shortDescription: A strange but useful guide to harbor beasts, dock pests, rare catches, and the awkward question of what they are worth alive, dead, salted, or loudly contained. Traders keep it close when fishermen arrive with something still moving in a barrel.
flavorText: “The beast is only half the problem. The other half is naming a price before it bites someone.”

name: Practical Charms for Rainy Markets
shortDescription: A pocket book of practical charms, habits, and superstitions for markets that must function in steady rain. It mixes folk magic with common sense, which is often the most reliable combination available under a leaking awning.
flavorText: “A dry purse is good luck. A dry ledger is good management.”
